payload.summary: All four amendments identify real defects or maintainability gaps. Two are provenance/correctness bugs that can permanently mislabel task-run lineage, one is an invariant-handling regression that collapses an error case into an undiagnosed None path, and one adds a needed regression test for a known drift-prone manual merge pattern.

votes.rationale: This amendment has merit because the code now conflates two different outcomes: a legitimate 'no unique match' result and an invariant violation where run_id is present without started_at. Returning bare None erases the reason for failure in release builds, which weakens diagnosability and undermines the stated requirement for runtime checks. A log or explicit error return would preserve the contract violation signal without relying on debug-only assertions.

The manual field-by-field merge in TaskRunEntry is structurally prone to omission when new optional fields are added. Without a guard, a future field can silently fail to merge and the defect may only surface as missing persisted data. A round-trip test comparing a fully populated secondary entry against the merged result is a low-cost, robust regression check for exactly this class of drift.

Auto-populating a new TaskRunEntry with the current snapshot hash when plan_hash is omitted is not safe. If the plan changes after dispatch but before start recording, the row is stamped with a newer hash than the one actually used to launch the work, permanently corrupting provenance and any downstream journal interpretation. The in-code comment already describes the race, so this is a known correctness bug rather than a speculative concern.

Treating snapshot_plan_hash_at_creation == None as proof that no plan existed is semantically unsound for legacy rows. Legacy entries can have None simply because the field did not exist yet, even though they were created under an earlier plan version. Backfilling such rows with the current snapshot hash rewrites historical lineage and defeats the intended provenance protection, so the amendment identifies a real data-corruption path.
